**Hunstanton** (sometimes pronounced [/ˈhʌnstən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Hunstanton.ogg "File:En-uk-Hunstanton.ogg")[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is a [seaside town](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort") and [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), England, which had a population of 4,370 at the 2021 census. It faces west across [The Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash"). Hunstanton lies 102 miles (164 km) north-north-east of [London](/wiki/London "London") and 40 miles (64 km) north-west of [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich").

## Etymology and pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and pronunciation")]

The original settlement, now [Old Hunstanton](/wiki/Old_Hunstanton "Old Hunstanton"), probably gained its name from the [River Hun](/wiki/River_Hun_(Norfolk) "River Hun (Norfolk)"), which runs to the coast just to the east. It has also been argued that the name originated from "Honeystone", referring to the local red [carrstone](/wiki/Carrstone "Carrstone").

The town's name is often pronounced "Hunstan";[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) "Hunston" (the emphasis is placed upon the vowel of the first syllable).[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Peter Trudgill](/wiki/Peter_Trudgill "Peter Trudgill") suggests that in the 8th Century CE Hunstanton was called *hunstans-tūn*, meaning 'the homestead of Hunstan', but that this three-syllable pronunciation "eventually and quite naturally acquired a more reduced form, *Hunst'nt'n*, which was later further reduced to *Hunst'n*". He writes that the original form would "most certainly not come down to us as *Hun-STAN-ton* - that is a modern mistake". The "authentic old three-syllable pronunciation" is, he asserts, "HUN-st'n-t'n"[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Hunstanton_Town_Hall_and_Le_Strange_statue_%28geograph_5686415%29.jpg/250px-Hunstanton_Town_Hall_and_Le_Strange_statue_%28geograph_5686415%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hunstanton_Town_Hall_and_Le_Strange_statue_(geograph_5686415).jpg)

[Hunstanton Town Hall](/wiki/Hunstanton_Town_Hall "Hunstanton Town Hall") and the statue of Henry Styleman Le Strange

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/St._Edmund%27s_Memorial_Chapel%2C_Hunstanton.jpg/250px-St._Edmund%27s_Memorial_Chapel%2C_Hunstanton.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Edmund%27s_Memorial_Chapel,_Hunstanton.jpg)

Remains of St Edmund's Memorial Chapel in 2016

Hunstanton is a 19th-century resort town, initially known as New Hunstanton to distinguish it from the adjacent village of that name. The new town soon exceeded the village in scale and population.

In 1846, [Henry L'Estrange Styleman Le Strange](/wiki/Henry_L%27Estrange_Styleman_Le_Strange "Henry L'Estrange Styleman Le Strange") (1815–1862),[[6]](#cite_note-6) decided to develop the area south of Old Hunstanton as a bathing resort. He brought a group of like-minded investors into building a railway line from [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn"). In 1861, Le Strange, as principal landowner, became a director of the railway company. By 1862 the line had been built. Le Strange died that year at the age of 47, leaving his son Hamon to reap the rewards of his efforts. The [Lynn and Hunstanton Railway](/wiki/Lynn_and_Hunstanton_Railway "Lynn and Hunstanton Railway") became one of the most consistently profitable in the country.

Le Strange moved the ancient village cross from Old Hunstanton to a new site in 1846. In 1848 the first main building, the Royal Hotel (now the *Golden Lion*), was built by Victorian architect, [William Butterfield](/wiki/William_Butterfield "William Butterfield"), a friend of Le Strange. Overlooking a sloping green and the sea, and for several years standing alone, it earned the nickname "Le Strange's Folly". In 1850 Le Strange, an amateur architect and painter, appointed a land agent to survey the site and prepare a plan. Le Strange drew and painted a map and a perspective of the scheme, showing shops, a station and a church. He consulted William Butterfield on the design. Their shared passion was for an "Old English" style of architecture for domestic buildings, owing much to medieval precedents and the earnest [Victorian Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture"). Hunstanton came to exemplify a 19th-century estate seaside town. Most of the fabric and character of that development survives.

In 1915, during the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), Hunstanton was the headquarters of the West Norfolk training programme of the [Queen's Own Cameron Highlanders](/wiki/Queen%27s_Own_Cameron_Highlanders "Queen's Own Cameron Highlanders"), as they prepared for [active service](/wiki/Active_service "Active service") on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Among them were regimental [bagpiper](/wiki/Great_Highland_bagpipe "Great Highland bagpipe") [Iain Eairdsidh MacAsgaill](/wiki/Iain_Eairdsidh_MacAsgaill "Iain Eairdsidh MacAsgaill") (1898-1934), and poet [Dòmhnall Ruadh Chorùna](/wiki/D%C3%B2mhnall_Ruadh_Chor%C3%B9na "Dòmhnall Ruadh Chorùna") (1887-1967).

Hunstanton was hit badly by the [North Sea flood of 1953](/wiki/North_Sea_flood_of_1953 "North Sea flood of 1953"). The wall of water on the night of 31 January – 1 February killed 31 people, 16 of them [United States military](/wiki/United_States_military "United States military") personnel and their families. There were 35 more victims in neighbouring [Snettisham](/wiki/Snettisham "Snettisham") and [Heacham](/wiki/Heacham "Heacham").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Governance")]

The Hunstanton [electoral ward](/wiki/Wards_and_electoral_divisions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Wards and electoral divisions of the United Kingdom") belongs to the Borough Council of [King's Lynn and West Norfolk](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_and_West_Norfolk "King's Lynn and West Norfolk").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Its 2011 population was 5,420.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Hunstanton has a mayor and a 17-member town council that meets twice a month at [Hunstanton Town Hall](/wiki/Hunstanton_Town_Hall "Hunstanton Town Hall").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The parish was formed as "New Hunstanton" in 1894 from "Hunstanton".[[12]](#cite_note-12) On 1 April 1974 "New Hunstanton" parish was renamed "Hunstanton" and "Hunstanton" parish renamed "Old Hunstanton".[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geology")]

Further information: [Hunstanton Cliffs](/wiki/Hunstanton_Cliffs "Hunstanton Cliffs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Hunstanton_Cliffs.jpg/330px-Hunstanton_Cliffs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hunstanton_Cliffs.jpg)

The stratified [carrstone](/wiki/Carrstone "Carrstone") (orange),[[17]](#cite_note-17) red chalk [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") and white [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") [cliffs](/wiki/Cliff "Cliff") on the beach at Old Hunstanton

The coastal cliffs include the [type section](/wiki/Type_section "Type section") of the [Hunstanton Formation](/wiki/Hunstanton_Formation "Hunstanton Formation") of lower reddish [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), which was laid down during the [Lower Cretaceous](/wiki/Lower_Cretaceous "Lower Cretaceous"). This is topped by a white [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") layer from the [Upper Cretaceous](/wiki/Upper_Cretaceous "Upper Cretaceous") period.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Hunstanton's summer crowds are smaller than in the 1980s, although its relative popularity with [day-trippers](/wiki/Day-tripper "Day-tripper") and holidaymakers has endured, despite the decline in British seaside holidaying. Businesses in villages south of Hunstanton ([Dersingham](/wiki/Dersingham "Dersingham"), [Ingoldisthorpe](/wiki/Ingoldisthorpe "Ingoldisthorpe") and [Snettisham](/wiki/Snettisham "Snettisham")) complained in the 1990s of a loss in trade after being bypassed by the [A149](/wiki/A149_road "A149 road") to Hunstanton.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Boat_on_the_Wash_at_Hunstanton.jpg/250px-Boat_on_the_Wash_at_Hunstanton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boat_on_the_Wash_at_Hunstanton.jpg)

Looking out across [The Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash") from Hunstanton

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/UK_Hunstanton_%28SideA%29.jpg/250px-UK_Hunstanton_%28SideA%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:UK_Hunstanton_(SideA).jpg)

Place name sign in Hunstanton

The town has several Victorian squares. Boston Square provides a view across [the Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash") to [Boston, Lincolnshire](/wiki/Boston,_Lincolnshire "Boston, Lincolnshire") where both [Boston Stump](/wiki/St_Botolph%27s_Church,_Boston "St Botolph's Church, Boston") and the seaside town of [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness") are visible on clear days.

Hunstanton has a fairground, aquarium and seal sanctuary, leisure pool, theatre, large caravan parks with amenities, some amusement arcades, and a long promenade. The centrepiece remains the large sloping green from one end of High Street to the promenade.

Hunstanton has markets on Wednesdays and Sundays selling fresh fish, and fresh fruit and vegetables, which attract greater visitor numbers in the summer months through to the autumn. The main shopping streets feature stone buildings, some with glazed canopies, evoking the [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") and [Edwardian eras](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era") of their construction.

In good weather, excursion boats take visitors out to view [grey](/wiki/Grey_seals "Grey seals") and [common seals](/wiki/Common_seal "Common seal") that have colonised [sand bars](/wiki/Sand_bar "Sand bar") in the Wash and to the north of Norfolk. The countryside around Hunstanton is hillier than most of Norfolk and sparsely populated; the only large settlement nearby is King's Lynn, 12 miles (19 km) to the south.

## Hunstanton Pier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hunstanton Pier")]

The town once had a Victorian [pleasure pier](/wiki/Pleasure_pier "Pleasure pier"), with a pavilion and [miniature steam railway](/wiki/Ridable_miniature_railway "Ridable miniature railway"). The pier was originally authorised by the **Hunstanton Pier Order 1868**. The pier pavilion was destroyed by fire in 1939, the pier was damaged by fire again in the 1950s, before almost the entire structure was washed away by a storm in 1978. What remained extended just 15 feet out from the amusement arcade and cafe built on the site of the original entrance. In 2002, the entire building, with the remains of the pier, was destroyed in a fire. The building was too badly damaged for the cause to be determined. Today, the site is occupied by an arcade and bowling alley complex.

The pier featured in the 1957 Ealing Studios comedy film *[Barnacle Bill](/wiki/Barnacle_Bill_(1957_film) "Barnacle Bill (1957 film)")* (released in the US as *All at Sea*) starring [Alec Guinness](/wiki/Alec_Guinness "Alec Guinness").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transport")]

The town is linked to King's Lynn by a frequent [Lynx](/wiki/Lynx_(bus_company) "Lynx (bus company)") bus service. Other services run to [Sandringham](/wiki/Sandringham,_Norfolk "Sandringham, Norfolk"), [Wells-next-the-Sea](/wiki/Wells-next-the-Sea "Wells-next-the-Sea"), [Sheringham](/wiki/Sheringham "Sheringham") and [Cromer](/wiki/Cromer "Cromer").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[Hunstanton railway station](/wiki/Hunstanton_railway_station "Hunstanton railway station") offered services to [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_railway_station "King's Lynn railway station") until 1969, when the line was closed as uneconomic.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]

The [Smithdon High School](/wiki/Smithdon_High_School "Smithdon High School") (formerly Hunstanton Secondary Modern School) is an early building designed by the architects [Peter and Alison Smithson](/wiki/Peter_and_Alison_Smithson "Peter and Alison Smithson"), built in 1949–1954 in a radical style of international architectural significance. It is a Grade II\* listed building.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The school epitomised architectural experiment in post-war Britain and growing acceptance of modernism by public authorities. It was praised for an intelligent layout and formal elegance.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Smithsons deliberately left many of the service elements of the school exposed, making a feature of the water tank by turning it into a tower. The disposition, steel frames and panels of brick and glass echo the work of [Mies van der Rohe](/wiki/Mies_van_der_Rohe "Mies van der Rohe") at the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Hunstanton is home to Glebe House School, an independent co-educational [preparatory school](/wiki/Preparatory_school_(UK) "Preparatory school (UK)").

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Culture")]

The Princess Theatre is a 472-seat, year-round venue for shows from comedy to drama, music for all tastes, and children's productions. It also has a six-week summer season and an annual Christmas pantomime. Films are screened in the week. Opened as the Capitol Cinema in 1932, it is noted for its Norfolk carrstone construction, of which it contains the largest gable wall in existence. It was designed as a theatre as well as a cinema, but closed in the 1960s and was sold in 1974. It reopened as the Kingsley Centre for summer seasons and films for about two years, but declined into a bingo hall before closing again. The Borough Council of King's Lynn and West Norfolk purchased it in 1981, and in honour of [Lady Diana Spencer](/wiki/Lady_Diana_Spencer "Lady Diana Spencer"), who married the Prince of Wales in July 1981, it was renamed the Princess Theatre and officially re-opened on 5 July 1981.

Hunstanton Concert Band plays in and around Hunstanton at a wide variety of venues including churches, fêtes, concerts and the town's bandstand. The [Deaf Havana](/wiki/Deaf_Havana "Deaf Havana") album *[Fools and Worthless Liars](/wiki/Fools_and_Worthless_Liars "Fools and Worthless Liars")* featured a track called "Hunstanton Pier", a nostalgic recollection of the town where James Veck-Gilodi, its lead singer, grew up.

### Literary associations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Literary associations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Wreck_of_the_Sheraton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1553402.jpg/250px-Wreck_of_the_Sheraton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1553402.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wreck_of_the_Sheraton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1553402.jpg)

Wreck of the *Sheraton*

Between the world wars, [P. G. Wodehouse](/wiki/P._G._Wodehouse "P. G. Wodehouse") often visited his friend Charles Le Strange at [Hunstanton Hall](/wiki/Hunstanton_Hall "Hunstanton Hall"). It influenced a number of locations in his comic novels, as [Aunt Agatha](/wiki/Aunt_Agatha "Aunt Agatha")'s country seat Woollam Chersey and the inspiration for the setting for [*Money for Nothing*](/wiki/Money_for_Nothing_(novel) "Money for Nothing (novel)") (1928). The octagon in the garden featured in ["Jeeves and the Impending Doom"](/wiki/Very_Good,_Jeeves "Very Good, Jeeves"). Norfolk also furnishes names for many of Wodehouse's characters, such as Brancaster, Jack Snettisham and J. Sheringham Adair.

[L. P. Hartley](/wiki/L._P._Hartley "L. P. Hartley") knew the Hunstanton neighbourhood from childhood holidays and used it as a setting for *The Shrimp and the Anemone* (1944), the first novel in his Eustace and Hilda trilogy. It is at Hunstanton Hall, fictionalised as Anchorstone Hall, that Eustace enters the privileged world of the aristocracy and eventually inherits a small fortune. The layered chalk, red chalk and carr-stone cliffs at Hunstanton provide a backdrop for Eustace and Hilda's games among the rock pools.

[Patrick Hamilton](/wiki/Patrick_Hamilton_(writer) "Patrick Hamilton (writer)")'s novel *[Hangover Square](/wiki/Hangover_Square "Hangover Square")* opens with George Harvey Bone walking on the cliffs in Hunstanton. Hamilton lived for many years at Martincross in [Sheringham](/wiki/Sheringham "Sheringham") and spent time in the 1930s in a cottage in [Burnham Overy Staithe](/wiki/Burnham_Overy_Staithe "Burnham Overy Staithe"), with his first wife, Lois.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Media")]

Regional TV services are provided by [BBC Yorkshire and Lincolnshire](/wiki/BBC_Yorkshire_and_Lincolnshire "BBC Yorkshire and Lincolnshire") and [ITV Yorkshire](/wiki/ITV_Yorkshire "ITV Yorkshire"). Television signals are received from the [Belmont TV transmitter](/wiki/Belmont_transmitting_station "Belmont transmitting station").[[25]](#cite_note-25) [BBC East](/wiki/BBC_East "BBC East") and [ITV Anglia](/wiki/ITV_Anglia "ITV Anglia") are also received through cable and satellite television such as [Freesat](/wiki/Freesat "Freesat") and [Sky](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK").

Local radio stations are [BBC Radio Norfolk](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Norfolk "BBC Radio Norfolk"), [Heart East](/wiki/Heart_East "Heart East"), [Greatest Hits Radio West Norfolk](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_East "Greatest Hits Radio East") (formerly [KL.FM 96.7](/wiki/KL.FM_96.7 "KL.FM 96.7")) *Radio West Norfolk* [[26]](#cite_note-26) and *KL1 Radio*.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The town is served by these local newspapers:

* *[Lynn News](/wiki/Lynn_News "Lynn News")*[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* *Your Local Paper* [[29]](#cite_note-29)
* [The Eastern Daily Press](/wiki/The_Eastern_Daily_Press "The Eastern Daily Press")[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Paragliding_over_Hunstanton_sea_cliffs.jpg/250px-Paragliding_over_Hunstanton_sea_cliffs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paragliding_over_Hunstanton_sea_cliffs.jpg)

Paragliding above the cliffs in Hunstanton

The ITA Hunstanton lawn tennis tournament has taken place in Hunstanton most summers since establishment in 1920.[[31]](#cite_note-31) With up to 1,300 competitors taking part each year its organisers claim that it the largest tennis tournament in the UK.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[Hunstanton Golf Club](/wiki/Hunstanton_Golf_Club "Hunstanton Golf Club"), founded in 1891 by Hamon Le Strange, is an 18-hole championship links along the sandy coast of Old Hunstanton. It has a classic "out and back" design on either side of a central spine or dune ridge. The 12th, 13th and 14th holes play across the ridge.

The town has hosted several international sporting events, including the 2005 World Water Ski Racing Championships.

Lifestyles Festival was a summer sports festival held annually in Hunstanton from 2005 until 2014. It specialised in sporting events such as expert [BMXing](/wiki/BMX_bike "BMX bike"), [in-line skating](/wiki/In-line_skating "In-line skating"), and [skateboarding](/wiki/Skateboarding "Skateboarding"), and [Kite Surfing](/wiki/Kite_Surfing "Kite Surfing"), along with cultural events including live music, visual arts, and dance. An estimated 12,000 people attended in 2010.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunstanton&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notable people")]

In birth order:

* [Edmund the Martyr](/wiki/Edmund_the_Martyr "Edmund the Martyr") (died 869), King of East Anglia, supposedly landed here to claim his kingdom about 855.
* Sir [Roger L'Estrange](/wiki/Roger_L%27Estrange "Roger L'Estrange") (1616–1704), [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") and [pamphleteer](/wiki/Pamphleteer "Pamphleteer"), was born here.
* [Guy Le Strange](/wiki/Guy_Le_Strange "Guy Le Strange") (1854–1933), [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") scholar and linguist, was born here.
* [George Grundy](/wiki/George_Grundy "George Grundy") (1859–1945), first-class cricketer, died here.
* [Tiverton Preedy](/wiki/Tiverton_Preedy "Tiverton Preedy") (1863–1928), Anglican cleric and sports promoter, was born here.
* [Clara Dow](/wiki/Clara_Dow "Clara Dow") (1883–1969), operatic soprano and actress, made her stage début here in 1899.
* [Robert Herring](/wiki/Robert_Herring_(RAF_officer) "Robert Herring (RAF officer)") (1897–1973), an officer in the [London Regiment](/wiki/London_Regiment_(1908%E2%80%931938) "London Regiment (1908–1938)"), a [flying ace](/wiki/Flying_ace "Flying ace") in the [Royal Flying Corps](/wiki/Royal_Flying_Corps "Royal Flying Corps"), and the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") during both World Wars, born here. Recipient of the [Military Cross](/wiki/Military_Cross "Military Cross")
* [Reis Leming](/wiki/Reis_Leming "Reis Leming") (1930–2012), US airman, received the UK [George Medal](/wiki/George_Medal "George Medal") for rescuing 27 people from South Beach during the North Sea flood of 1953.
* [Richard Greer](/wiki/Richard_Greer "Richard Greer") (born 1946), motorcycle speedway rider in the 1970s and 1980s, lives here.
* [Bill Alexander](/wiki/Bill_Alexander_(director) "Bill Alexander (director)") (born 1948), theatre director, was born here.
